one_line_takeaway: SKeDA embeds a 256-bit watermark in the initial latent noise of a text-to-video diffusion model using a shuffle-permuted shared random sequence (SKe) plus a difference-based frame-reweighting extractor (DA), improving bit-accuracy robustness to common video distortions like H.264 compression, frame drop, and frame swap by roughly 5-20% over five image/video watermarking baselines while also improving FVD/CLIP-score/VBench visual-quality metrics.
key_finding: SKeDA improves robustness under video-specific compression scenarios (H.264/H.265) by 5-20% over mainstream baselines per the paper's own summary, while matching or improving visual fidelity. Concretely from Table II (bit accuracy at FPR=1e-6, averaged over all seven distortions): SKeDA=0.9853 vs. REVMark=0.9738, WAM=0.9717, Video Seal=0.9632, DVMark=0.9579, HiDDeN=0.8662. The largest single-distortion gain is under H.264 (CRF=30) compression: SKeDA=0.9687 vs. the next-best WAM=0.8612 and REVMark=0.8577. Table I shows SKeDA also achieves the best (lowest) FVD of 361.3 (vs. DVMark 382.8, HiDDeN 436.1), best CLIP-score of 0.3345 (vs. REVMark 0.3287, WAM 0.3146), and best Video Quality of 0.7898 (vs. REVMark 0.7834 as cited in text, though Table I lists REVMark's Video Quality as 0.7884 -- a discrepancy the paper itself does not reconcile). The ablation (Table VII) shows the DA module raises average accuracy from 0.9039 to 0.9564 (video-distortion accuracy 0.9431→0.9853, image-distortion accuracy 0.8647→0.9274), isolating its contribution independent of the SKe module.
core_contribution: SKeDA, a generative watermarking framework for text-to-video diffusion models (built on Stable Diffusion 1.5 + AnimateDiff's motion module) that embeds a watermark directly into the initial latent noise rather than post-hoc into pixels. It has two novel components: (1) Shuffle-Key-based Distribution-preserving Sampling (SKe), which derives all per-frame pseudo-random encryption sequences as permutations of a single shared base binary sequence (rather than independent frame-dependent sequences as in prior generative image watermarking extended naively to video). This converts watermark extraction from synchronization-sensitive sequence decoding into permutation-tolerant set-level aggregation, so extraction survives frame reordering/dropping while still preserving the Gaussian sampling distribution the diffusion model expects. (2) Differential Attention (DA), an extraction-time module that computes inter-frame differences to identify temporally stable regions and adaptively reweights each frame's contribution during DDIM-style inversion, instead of averaging all frames equally.
threat_model: The paper's motivating scenario (Fig. 1) is copyright/provenance attribution: a generative-video service provider wants to (a) detect which model produced a given video and (b) trace which user/prompt generated it, including catching an adversary who steals another user's watermarked output. The 'attacks' evaluated are common video-processing operations that could destroy or desynchronize the watermark rather than an adaptive/white-box adversary: frame averaging (N=3), frame drop (p=0.5), frame swap (p=0.5), random crop (p=0.7), Gaussian noise (std=0.04), Gaussian blur (std=2.0), H.264 compression (CRF=30), plus in the ablation JPEG, resize, median filtering, salt-and-pepper noise, and varying crop/drop ratios. This is a robustness-against-transformations threat model typical of watermarking papers, not a study of adaptive watermark-removal attackers or model-security vulnerabilities.
limitations: SKeDA does not uniformly win on every individual distortion: under Random Crop (p=0.7) WAM scores higher (0.9912 vs. SKeDA's 0.9585), and under Gaussian Noise Video Seal is essentially tied/slightly higher (0.9887 vs. 0.9890 is close but WAM 0.9822/DVMark 0.9901 are competitive too) -- SKeDA's advantage is concentrated in compression and frame-manipulation distortions (drop, swap, H.264), not crop. All evaluation is on 2-second, 16-frame, 512x512 clips generated by one specific SD1.5+AnimateDiff pipeline; the paper explicitly states future work is needed to extend to larger watermark capacities, longer video sequences, and more complex generation scenarios, implying the current results may not generalize to longer or higher-resolution video or to other T2V backbones (e.g., newer DiT-based video models). The 'robustness' evaluation is against common signal-processing-style distortions, not against an adaptive adversary who knows the watermarking scheme and optimizes to remove it, so no security guarantee against a motivated evasion attacker is established. The Table I vs. in-text Video Quality figure for REVMark (0.7884 vs. 0.7834) is an internal inconsistency in the paper's own reporting.

datasets_used: WebVid-10M (subset used for FVD/CLIP-score/Video-Quality evaluation; 1000 videos for FVD, 10 batches of 100 videos for aggregate metrics)
quantitative_results: Table I (visual quality): SKeDA FVD=361.3 (lower better) vs. DVMark 382.8, HiDDeN 436.1, REVMark 369.3, Video Seal 365.1, WAM 379.2
Table I: SKeDA CLIP-score=0.3345 vs. REVMark 0.3287, WAM 0.3146, Video Seal 0.3302, DVMark 0.3274, HiDDeN 0.3077
Table I: SKeDA Video Quality=0.7898 vs. Video Seal 0.7799, REVMark 0.7884, WAM 0.7846, DVMark 0.7742, HiDDeN 0.7436
Table II (bit accuracy at FPR=1e-6, average over 7 distortions): SKeDA=0.9853, REVMark=0.9738, WAM=0.9717, Video Seal=0.9632, DVMark=0.9579, HiDDeN=0.8662
Table II, H.264 CRF=30 specifically: SKeDA=0.9687 vs. WAM=0.8612, REVMark=0.8577, Video Seal=0.8556, DVMark=0.7996, HiDDeN=0.7008
Watermark payload capacity: SKeDA=256 bits, WAM=32 bits, HiDDeN/REVMark/Video Seal/DVMark=96 bits
Table VI (sampler ablation, bit accuracy): DPMSolver=0.9992 (best), UniPC=0.9989, DEIS=0.9983, DDIM=0.9983, PNDM=0.9822

Table VII (DA module ablation): without DA -- video-distortion accuracy 0.9431, image-distortion accuracy 0.8647, average accuracy 0.9039; with DA -- 0.9853, 0.9274, 0.9564 respectively (TPR unaffected at 1.0000 in both cases)
